description: A14FJ - Day 8, part 2: Flashing Lights Experiment and Probe Stowage

tokens: An extra scientific goal has been set up for the eighth day of the mission - an experiment to characterise the strange flashes of light that have been observed by past Apollo crews.
This would be the first systematic attempt to study the phenomenon that has puzzled crews and scientists alike.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Editor's note:All transcript times are presented according to the GET update at 054:53:36 that saw the mission timer moved forward 40 minutes, 2.90 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay; back to our original question.
Under the assumption that we are not yet ready to stow the probe, would you all be agreeable to trying some of this light-flash observation, at the present time or after you finish eating?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We'll go ahead and do it now, if you'd like.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Do we want to use the - DSE, the voice loop, or how do you want us to record this info?
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: I think we might as well use the voice loop.
You can just send it down under the assumption that we're on good comm here.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The objectives stated for this period of observations are, first off, to confirm that it is not necessary to be dark-adapted to see the flashes; secondly, to collect the real-time data on the times of occurrence of different types of flash; and, lastly, to determine if there's any other sensory effect associated with this phenomenon.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You're requested to - you're requested to become thoroughly light-adapted, that is, by looking at a flashlight.
Then relax, start the dark-adaptation procedure, and report and describe flashes as they occur.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We lost you right after you started talking about the procedure.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: You should configure the cabin so that you can, indeed, start the dark-adaptation procedure.
And in starting the procedure, you are asked to become thoroughly light-adapted, as by looking at a flashlight or floodlight.
Then relax, start the dark adaptation, and report and describe flashes as they occur.
Marking the time of the occurrence of the flash takes priority over detailed description.
One of you should continue observations, dark-adapted, as long as possible.
The others should - expose themselves to light again after about 25 minutes, and we'll give you a cue on that.

And repeat the experiment for another 10 minutes -as many cycles as you feel you have time for, in order to increase our probability of getting flash observations during the early portion of the dark-adaptation transients.
We?d also like to hear from you commentary on any other unusual physical sensations , such as tingling or apparent sounds of unusual character - like ringing in the ears, and so forth.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: How did all that get into it?
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Well, it's what our investigator has decided he's interested in.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I mean, these things aren't timebombs going off in your head, you know.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: And along those lines, he's looking for the following data on the flashes: the type of flash, i.e., star streak, nova, et cetera; brightness; the location in your visual field; color, if any; and whether it's possible to distinguish or not which eye the flash seems to occur in.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And let us know when you start dark-adapting each time, and we'll time it for you.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We have the - window shades in now.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: And, just for reference, we're playing back the video tapes of your last evening's - inflight demonstration TV show for about the fifth time down here.
I think you're going to be a best seller.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Okay, Houston, let's review what we intend to do here just a second.
Okay, we have all three on now, and we're going to - we have the window shades in; the cabin lights are currently on.
At T^, all three of us shine a flashlight in our eyes, turn out all the lights, and that's the start of the sequence.
Stu and I'll recycle about once every 10 minutes, and Ed keeps going as long as he can stay awake.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: That's correct, except that on the first batch we'd like to keep you all in the dark-adaptation procedure for about 25 minutes and then have two of you start recycling at 10-minute intervals .
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Understand you want all three for 25 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: One crewman, whom you can determine amongst you, should, when he starts the dark-adaptation procedure, continue in the dark-adapted condition until the termination of the experiment.
The other two of you should do this light-adaptation again after 25 minutes and then recycle on a 10-minute basis.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Well, it looks to me like we'll probably have time for one 25-minute cycle.
We have a P52 coming up here at 192:10.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: We see that in the Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: All right, all three of us will go for 25 minutes and Ed'll continue on; and we'll discuss how the other two will go from there.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Give me long enough to get the flashlights ready.
We'll give you a mark here in about 40 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Okay, Tq will be 191:20:00, Here we go.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Not only correct, but unusual, Bruce.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Run that one by again, please, Ed.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I say it's unusual; I think most of us see them long before now.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Well, if we don't see anything by 20 minutes, I suggest we terminate - and try to give you some more qualitative information during tonight's sleep period.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: A faint star - left eye at - 10 o'clock.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Faint star, left eye, at 10 o'clock.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: High and left to lower right.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: You have a color on any of these?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Do you have a color on these?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: These have both been white so far.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We all have a consensus that we've never seen anything that's colored.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: It is a cloud at 12 o'clock, high, left eye.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Right eye, 8 o'clock, low, single flash.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: How's the redheaded cosmic ray detector doing?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Not - must not be my time for starry
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: He's shielded by the nav base.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: The navigational optics are fitted onto a sturdy structure made out of aluminum and filled with polyurethane foam.
It could very well have a shielding effect for particles coming from behind it.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 10 o'clock - about level; it was a - a double star.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: And it was the left eye; and, believe it or not, it had kind of a bluish tint to it.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: It was - it looked more like a blue diamond.
You know, very white, but with a blue cast.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Streak, left eye, 12 o'clock, low; and it was going from the bottom toward the top.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Twenty-three minutes and 34 seconds into the test.
And at 25 minutes, we would like Ed, and Ed only, to shine the flashlight in his eyes long enough to ruin his dark adaptation, and then start the dark-adaptation procedure again.
We'd like to be sure that you don't shine the flashlight in anybody else's eyes.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Well, they'll just have to close their eyes then, I guess.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I'll give you a hack at 25 minutes.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Only been seeing on my left eye.
Should I just dark-adapt that one?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Low at 6 o'clock, and it was a double flash, left eye.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Let's not - let's not go halfway.
You can - light up both your eyeballs.
And - we're 24 minutes 50 seconds and counting.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Twenty-five minutes, and let us know when you turn the light back out.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I don't think I'll see anything for days after that one.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Right eye at - It'd be 3 o'clock, low, and it was a streak; went from right to left.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: That was about 51 seconds when you called mark.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Left eye - Yes, I had a mark right in the middle of that conversation.
Left eye, 12 o'clock high, single flash.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Left eye, streaked from 6 o'clock to the center, going up.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Right eye, 4 o'clock - pretty much on the periphery; it was a flash.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Right eye, nova, right in the middle.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: The crew has been observing light flashes for an experimental observation that began about 30 minutes ago and is scheduled to continue for about another 30 minutes reporting - observing of reporting observable light flashes which have been atributed to cosmic rays.

As they give a mark, experimenters in the science support room here in the control center note the time of it and a few seconds later the crewman describes the observation of the type and location as it was viewed in the eye and so far we've heard from Shepard and Mitchell.
I haven't detected any reports from Command Module Pilot, Stu Roosa.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Right eye, 11 o'clock, on the periphery, faint flash.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Right eye - Mark, right eye, about 5 o'clock halfway up, flash.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Simultaneous with Stu, in the left eye - cloud at 6 o'clock.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Right eye, 3 o'clock with a flash on the periphery.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Seven o'clock, flash on the periphery.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Simultaneous with Stu, Al had a - a lighting behind cloud, low, right eye.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Right eye, 4 o'clock, in the periphery, a faint nova.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Say again the last part of that, Ed.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: During that one, I had a cloud at 11 o'clock.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Left eye, 9 o'clock, down at the bottom.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: We?d like you to repeat the exposure of your li - eyeballs to light cycle and then start readapting again.
We want Stu and Al to continue in the dark-adapted condition.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: How much longer do you project this to run, Bruce?
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: I wonder if that was Ed's flashlight.
How much longer you think you're going to be running this test?
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: A total of about 10 minutes more.
And then we'll come out, and Stu can start the P52 and we'll be back in business.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Currently, I've got 191 plus 57, or 13 minutes , until the block in the Flight Plan for starting the P52.
So I think we're still in good shape.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Right eye, streak; it's at the center going out, 3 o'clock.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Flash, at 5 o'clock, about half way out.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Left eye, 12 o'clock, high, a flash.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Nova, right eye, low - 12 o'clock, low..
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: For your documentation on spacecraft orientation, Al is in the - left couch looking up; Ed is in the right couch looking up; and Stu's in the LEB facing Mecca.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Stu is in the LEB facing the nav base?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I'm - I'm floating in LEB, Bruce.
But it's - I'm looking toward plus-X.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We couldn't find Mecca on the stowage list there.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Flash, right eye, at 12 o'clock, level.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Streak, right eye, from upper right to the center.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We have a - a query here, wondering how long, in terms of angular measurement, those streaks are?
I should say, apparent angular measurement.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Mine are abo - mine have been about half an eyeball diameter from - -
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Double shot in the right eye, at 10 o'clock and 7 o'clock.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Double shot in the right eye, 10 o'clock and center.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Flash at the same time, right eye, at 2 o'clock.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Streak, right on the periphery, at 3 o'clock, left eye, going vertical, bottom to top.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Bright flash, right eye, at about 1 o'clock, high.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Flash, at 4 o'clock, about halfway out, right eye.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Flash, 2 o'clock, on the periphery , right eye.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We're going to terminate the light-flash investigation at this time.
We would like to convey our appreciation to all three of you for cooperating in the investigation.
The investigators are very happy with the data that you have given them, and I think they will be able to get something significant out of it.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Flash, at 1 o'clock, on the periphery.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We can terminate the experiment and proceed with the P52, Stu.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And, Bruce, I'd just like to make a comment.
I don't know whether it's my position down in LEB or why, but even with those up in the couches, that's not near as many flashes as I've seen at other times.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And I was going to remark, Bruce, that most of the times when I've observed them have been after sleep.
Generally, I guess I fall asleep so fast when we turn in that there's not time to observe them.
But they always seem very bright, much brighter than right now - than they were this pass.
I don't know whether more numerous - perhaps more numerous - but always brighter than they were today.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: He concurs on that, he said.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I said on the intercom that I agree to that, also, Bruce.
Like last night - I was laying there and there were just, you know, big bright ones all over the place.
And these, seemed like, were a little subdued compared to that.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Well, let's see, that should put us at about 104 percent completion of the flight-plan items.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: I'll run that one by the FAO, and see what he's got to say.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We have canceled midcourse correction number 6 at this time.
Based on our tracking, the preliminary estimates are that midcourse 7 will not be required.
However, we won't be able to make that decision with certainty until tomorrow.
Based on MSFN tracking, your gamma at entry interface is minus 6.35 degrees.
Based on your onboard navigated state vector, it's minus 6.68.
So we're showing very good agreement there.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Say the MSFN angle again, Bruce.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: The MSFN angle is minus 6.35- The angle derived from your onboard navigated state vector is minus 6.68.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: These should come together as the marks continue, shouldn't they?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay, Bruce, and we'll torque at 192:14:25.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Run that time by again, please.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 192 hours 18 minutes.
Apollo 14 now 112,226 nautical miles from Earth traveling at a speed of 5180 feet per second or just a little bit under 1 mile per second.
At 3 pm today in the news center auditorium, there will be an Apollo 14 science briefing.
Those participating will be Lee R.
Scherer, NASA's Director of Apollo Lunar Exploration; Dr.
Simmons, the Manned Spacecraft Center's Chief Scientist; Dr.
Gast, Chief of the Manned Spacecraft Center's Lunar Planetary Sciences Division; Dr.
Robin Brett, Chief of the Geochemistry Branch at the Manned Spacecraft Center; Dr.
Geological Survey, who is a principal investigator on Apollo 14 for lunar field geology experiment.
This briefing, again, at 3 pm in the MSC auditorium - the main auditorium in building 1.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 192 hours 46 minutes.
A press conference on Apollo 14 Lunar Science is scheduled to begin at this time in the MSC building number 1 main auditorium.
We'll switch to that press conference now and record Apollo 14 air to ground for play back following the press conference.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We would like you to go to an attitude for thermal control, not PTC, and maintain that attitude until beginning of the TV coverage for the press conference; and that attitude is roll, 262; pitch, 090; yaw, 000.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Request you terminate the waste water dump now in progress.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Yes, Bruce; we've turned it off now.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: When you can give us P00 and Accept, we have a state vector and a clock update for you.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Maybe it works better if I talk into the mike.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: That comes through a lot better.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And, for your information, Stu, based On your last set of P23s, your navigated state vector has a gamma at entry interface of minus 6.74; MSFN solution of minus 6.35 remains unchanged.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Well, didn't sound like it helped us much.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Well, you might be right; who knows?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Observe the High Gain starting to oscillate.
What would you like to do with it?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Yes; they tell me you're on Omni Delta right now.
We'll get you something to do with the High Gain.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: If we're on Omni Delta, It's news to me.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We've uplink commanded you to Omni Delta.
We'd like you to park the High Gain Antenna at Pitch, minus 52; Yaw, 270.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And I have a brief flight-plan update for you, when you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Do not go into PTC until a GET of 198 plus 45.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Do not go to PTC until 198 plus 45.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: At 194 plus 50, a Verb 49 maneuver to the TV attitude, which is roll, 325, 090, 000.
In that attitude, High Gain Antenna, Pitch, plus 32; Yaw, 270.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: At - 194 plus 50: Verb 49 to TV attitude; pitch is 325, 090, 000, with High Gain - angles of 32 and 270.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: And - TV pass is scheduled from 195 plus 07 through 195 plus 37- Over.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: That completes the flight-plan update, and the computer's yours.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The computer's mine, and how about me going to Wide Deadband here - while we're setting here and until after the TV show.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 193 hours, 46 minutes.
During the press conference we accumulated about 3 to 4 minutes of taped conversation.
We'll be prepared to play that back.

The primary activity aboard the spacecraft during the press conference involved Program 23, mid-course navigational sightings using the spacecraft sextant and we'll play back the tape at this time before continuing to stand by live.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: - - an entry pad here for you shortly.
And then we'll be ready to start in on the great probe stowage exercise.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I have an entry PAD and a CSM state vector PAD for you.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We're ready to copy the entry PAD, Bruce.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Mid-Pacific entry PAD: roll, 000, 154, 000; 216:10:47, 267; minus 27.02, minus 172.64; max g 06.1; 36170, 6.35; 1135.0, 36251; RRT 216:27:47; 00:29; Noun 69 is NA; D0, 4.00, 02:10; 00:l8, 03:36, 08:03; sextant star 30, 354.3, 30.7; boresight star, good ol' Nu Hydra, up 21.1, left 2.8; up lift vector.

Comment 1: use nonexit EMS pattern; 2: Moon check attitude, Moon at left of window; roll, 000; pitch, 182; yaw, 000; number 3: Moonset GET, 216 plus 25 plus 15; number 4: RET of 90,000 feet, 6 plus 25; main deploy, 8 plus 51; landing, 13 plus 48; number 5: constant g entry is roll right - right; 6: GDC Align, Sirius and Rigel, 322, 325, 018; number 7: the attitude at .05g and the GDC Align angles assume a P52 nominal alignment, option 2, using the RRT time of 216 plus 27 plus 47.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Think the only thing I missed was the - the boresight star angles.
I have a up 21.1, and a left - something.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: And use nonexit EMS pattern; Moon check attitude is roll, 000; pitch, 182; yaw, 000; Moon will be to the left window; the Moonset is at 216:25:15; the RET of 90 K is 6 plus 25.

The mains is 8 plus 51, and landing is 13 plus 48; for the constant g reentry, it's roll right; GDC Align stars are Sirius and Rigel at angles of 332, 325, 018; and the Moonset and Moon check times, I believe you said, are for a P52, using option 3 - option 2, and RRT time of 216:27:47.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: There are two reference points around which the entry is calculated.
The first is the arbitrary altitude of 400,000 feet (121.92 kilometres).
This is known as Entry Interface and is used for trajectory calculations.
The second point is when the deceleration experienced by the Command Module reaches one-twentieth of a g (or 0.05g).
This is detected by the accelerometers in the G&N system and it therefore depends on the atmospheric conditions at the time.
The 0.05g timing can only be guessed at but it is important because it triggers both the progression of the computer programs that fly the entry, and the operation of the Entry Monitor System (EMS).
The crew use the EMS to keep an eye on the progress of an automatic entry and it gives them the necessary cues to fly the entry manually if they need to.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The data passed up for the Entry PAD is interpreted as follows:Landing target area:The landing target is the Mid-Pacific recovery area.IMU gimbal angles required for trim at 0.05g:Roll, 0°; pitch, 154°; yaw, 0°.
This will be the spacecraft's attitude relative to the IMU at the moment they reach a deceleration of twentieth of a g.
As aerodynamic forces build up, the CM will increasingly hold itself at a similar attitude.Time of the horizon check:216 hours, 10 minutes, 47 seconds GET.

This check will occur 17 minutes before time of Entry Interface which means that the latter will occur at 216:27:47.Spacecraft pitch at horizon check:267°.Splashdown point:27.02° south latitude, 172.64 west longitude.Maximum number of g's during entry:6.1.

Since the acceleration of one g is 9.81 m/s2, the crew will experience a deceleration of 59.8 m/s2.Velocity at Entry Interface (400,000 feet altitude, Noun 60):36,170 feet/second (11,025 m/s).Entry flight path angle at Entry Interface:6.35°.Range to go to splashdown point from 0.05g event:1,135.0 nautical miles.
To set up their EMS (Entry Monitor System) before re-entry, the crew need to know the expected distance the CM would travel from the 0.05 g event to landing.
This figure will be decremented by the EMS based on signals from its own accelerometer.
Since this is just a number to be entered into a counter, a SI conversion is not considered necessary.Predicted inertial velocity at 0.05g event:36,251 feet/second.
This is another entry for the EMS.
It is entered into the unit's Delta-V counter and will be decremented based on signals from its own accelerometer.

It is interesting to note that their velocity is expected to continue increasing between EI and the 0.05g event.Time of Entry Interface:216 hours, 27 minutes, 47 seconds GET.Time from Entry Interface to 0.05g event:00.29 (seconds).Planned drag level (deceleration) during the constant g phase (D0):4.00 g.Time from Entry Interface until their velocity slows sufficiently to allow a circular orbit around the Earth:02 minutes, 10 seconds.
The practical implication of this is that this is the "capture point" where the CM will no longer be able to skip off the atmosphere.

Since the spacecraft will already be within the Earth's sensible atmosphere at this point, drag will continue to slow the spacecraft and the return to Earth is assured.Time from Entry Interface that the communications blackout begins:00:18.Time from Entry Interface that the communications blackout ends:03:36.Time from Entry Interface that the drogue parachutes will deploy:08:03.Sextant star:30 (Menkent, Theta Centauri)Sextant shaft angle at Entry Interface minus 2 minutes:354.3°.Sextant trunnion angle at Entry Interface minus 2 minutes:30.7°.The next three items refer to an additional attitude check made using the COAS sighted on a star two minutes before Entry Interface.Boresight star:Nu Hydra (Nu Hydrae, also known as 4 Crateris)Boresight Star pitch angle on COAS:up 21.1°.Boresight Star X position on COAS:left 2.8°.Further information pertaining to the reentry is given in the following notes:Lift vector at Entry Interface:Up.
Since the direction of the lift vector is towards the crew's feet, they will re-enter in a heads-down attitude.Mooncheck:Moon visible at left window.

Moon viewing attitude roll, 0°; pitch, 182°; yaw, 0° - yet another attitude check by viewing the Moon at a particular time and attitude.Moonset:216:25:15 GETElapsed time at 90,000-ft altitude:06:25.Main parachute deployment time:08:51.Landing:13:48Constant g reentry roll:Right.GDC Align stars:The stars to be used for GDC Align purposes are Sirius (15) and Rigel (12).GDC Align angles:The align angles are roll, 332°; pitch, 325°; yaw, 18°.Comments in addition to the PAD are that the Non-exit EMS pattern is used.
Attitude references are made in regards to the IMU being aligned to the reentry REFSMMAT.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The attitudes that I was referencing you were the .05g attitude and the GDC Align angles.
Your statements regarding Moonset and all that, however, are also correct.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And for Stuart, I've got a CSM state vector PAD, if you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I'm ready with the P27 state vector PAD, now.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: It's a Verb 71, the index is 21.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Before I start, give me a time on this.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: It's GET of 200 hours even; that's 200 plus 00 plus 00.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I was reading you before, and I have my volume all the way turned up here.
Now, I really read you, Stu.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I just realized that my mike had -wasn't right.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We're ready to go on the probe stowage at the present time.
However, it was our intent to give you about a half an hour of free time prior to commencing the TV pass for the press conference to let you get configured in whatever mode you want up there.
So, we've got a couple of breakpoints in this procedure; and, if you concur, we could start into this and give it about 30-minutes' worth and then knock it off for the TV show.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: About all we have to do to configure for the TV show is to get the camera set up.
And, actually, Stu and I will be stowing the probe, and Ed could be setting up the camera, so we can work simultaneously.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: 14, this is Houston, As a preliminary to the probe stowage proper, we'd like to review a few stowage items for entry with you.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Okay, you might get up a little closer - -
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: do all this - Can do all this in real time, now.
We don't have to write anything down.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: The first few steps will be probably informational rather than anything else; and then, we'll get into the real-time stuff.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: It's our understanding that you have two decontamination bags at the present time, one weighing about 20 pounds and one weighing about 30 pounds.
The 30-pound decontamination bag and contents should be stowed on top of locker Alpha-13 for entry, using the existing tiedown rings.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Give us about 5 minutes, Bruce, and we'll have the A-10 in shape for you here.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Before you do too much with the A-10 situation, it's our understanding that you currently have a 20-pound bag on top of A-10; and we're going to ask you to move that over to the top of A?8 for entry.

And, of course, once you lash this down on top of A-8, which is not necessary to do right now, you're locking your inflight demo, the composite casting, into place there; and you want to be sure and get as far through that as it is your intention to do prior to lashing down A-8.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Now, let's go back to the bag that you want on A-13.
Make sure I've got that one.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We want the 30-pound decontamination bag on top of A-13 for entry.
It is not necessary to lash this down at the present time unless you so desire.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We'll put the 30-pounder on top of A-13, and we?ll lash it down at our convenience.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And I guess we're ready to press when you are, Bruce.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Then, we're going to have the 20-pound decontamination bag on top of A-8 for entry; and again, it's not necessary to lash this down yet.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Next, it's intended to stow the IV pressure garment assembly, that is, yours, with its helmet in a sleeping bag on top of the 28 - on top of the 20-pound bag on A-8.
Use LM webbing and the D-rings on the container for tiedowns.
Once again this can be delayed.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: IV refers to "intravehicular", meaning the lighter version of the Apollo A7L spacesuit worn by Stu.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And we'll put my suit, with helmet, gloves in a sleeping bag and tie them on top of the 20-pounder on A-8.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 194 hours 15 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
The crew of Apollo 14 now swiveling the high gain antenna around for a lock on with the Earth.
In this case it'll be the Goldstone station Just coming into acquisition at this time.
Preparing for the television pass to begin in about 7 minutes.
This particular television pass will be a Space Press Conference.
Fourteen questions have been submitted by newsmen covering the Apollo 14 Mission.
They will be read up to the crew by Spacecraft Communicator Gordon Fullerton, who is Just at this moment taking over the Capcom console.
As a matter of fact, the TV pass starts right at the shift handover when Gerry Griffin's team of Flight Controllers, the Gold Team, takes over from the outgoing maroon team headed up by Milt Windler.
Still a lot of noise on the circuit as we wait for the high gain antenna to acquire and get a solid lock on.
We'll leave the circuit up live at this time and follow the communications and the subsequent television.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Then, after that's all through, you need to verify a minimal 4-inch clearance to the couch.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Now then, we need you in real time here to go through locker Alpha-10 and remove anything that may be needed later on in the flight - prior to entry, such as 70-millimeter magazines, Reseau camera, or whatever you feel you'll require.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And, Stu, one item that we'd like you to remove from A-10 at this time is the bag that contains the hoses for the liquid-transfer demonstration.
And that's - that's just the bag because we're going to place the bag over the probe head later on and tape it in place; so, you'd want to leave the hoses in A-10.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: A-10 is secured to our satisfaction.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And now, in locker Alpha-5, we'd like you to remove the headrest pads and install them on the couches.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Also, the heel clips and five ropes, while you're down there temporarily.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You've got the heel clips and the ropes and the headrest pads out and installed on the couch.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And we want everything else removed from the right-hand side of A-5, that is, the cushion and added equipment that's in there and stow that in Bravo-1, the main food locker there.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Now, we'd like you to remove the two lithium hydroxide canisters from Alpha-6 and place them in the left-hand side of Alpha-5.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And while you're over there in Alpha-6, you might as well get all the rest of the stuff, the TV equipment, out of it.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Now, you want the cushion out of the right side of A-6.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: It's not required that you take the cushion out of the right-hand side of - or out of A-6.
We do want the TV gear out.

Ultimately, we're going to stow the TV equipment from Alpha-6 in the Bravo-1 food locker with the monitor wrapped in a constant wear garment; but since we're coining up on the press conference, you want to keep it out for operation at the present time.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: So, we now have A-6 completely empty, except for the cushion in the right side.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And Alpha-5 ought to have two lithium hydroxide canisters in the left-hand side of it, and the right-hand side of it ought to be completely empty.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Now, we need somebody that is qualified to tie knots.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: As I said before, we've got two sailors.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Al is a Captain in the United States Navy, while Ed holds the rank of a Commander.
Stu is an US Air Force Major.
McCandless himself is a Lieutenant Commander in the US Navy.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We want you to double one of the sleep-restraint ropes and to tie it to the plus-Y, plus-Z footpad on the aft bulkhead belonging to locker Alpha-6.
As a preliminary, you should remove the urine bag from under Alpha-6 to allow you to get under there with one hand.

You can put a hand underneath and a hand around the side toward panel 250, and this is the plus-Y, plus-Z-ward footpad of Alpha-6, and it's the one that's closest to panel 250 down there in the corner.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: So we should take the bag completely out from underneath, right?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: You can restow that back in there after you get the rope tied on, but it makes access a lot easier if it's out for this step.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And you want to - to tie the knot, using as little of the line as is convenient, probably a bowline or something like that to - the thing going to come up and tie around part of the probe.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Do you want this thing outside the footpad or inside the footpad?
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: We would like it trending through the space between the Alpha-6 locker and the waste management panel, so I guess it - that would probably be outside.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: On the loose end of this line, do you want a bight or do you want just the bitter ends?
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: On the loose end of it, Al, which is really a double end as I assume you're tying it on, there is no particular requirement.
You're Just going to tie the - the doubled-up piece of line around a part of the probe later on.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Then, we want you to take the three ropes that you've removed from A-5 there and tie one end of all three, either collectively or one at a time, to the A-6 aft bulkhead footpad; that's in the plus-Y, minus-Z direction.
That's the one on the box there that's farther - It's closer to the side hatch than the one you Just tied the one doubled-up rope around.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You want three ropes, the ends tied around that one.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: You're going to come off of there with effectively a triple strand of line, the full length.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We've got three more snakes on the loose.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Now that you've got the additional snakes tied to Alpha-6, you can put the urine bag back underneath it, unless you anticipate needing this assembly prior to entry.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We'll put it back underneath it.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: All right, now, we'd like to move over to the probe and ensure that the probe - umbilical power connectors are connected to the shorting plugs and that the probe is fully folded.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Is that an in work or an accomplished?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Well, I verified those - those steps, but we're going to have to stand by right now, here.
We're going to have to get the probe down out of the tunnel.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Ask ole 21 Nancy there if he'll verify that the probe connectors are connected to the shorting plugs and that the probe is fully folded.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Well, the umbilicals are connected to the shorting plugs, and I don't know if there's something in there that's fully folded; you know, the probe's folded up as you bring it out of the - out of the tunnel.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: I haven't done anything other than just - just a normal probe removal.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Yes, you might check to make sure that the old Teflon block there on the side is against the ratchet housing in - on the arm there that engages in the tunnel.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Bruce, are you waiting on us?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We'd like you to take two of the nominal 8-and-a-half-by-ll-size Flight Data File Books and tape them to the base or the capture latch release handle under the probe.
We've got tape in R-6, and it's your option as to which pubs you use; probably, the CSM Rescue Book and the CMP Solo Book or the Lunar Landmark Maps would be good candidates.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And, just by way of information, when we get this stowed, these pubs are going to come to rest against the right-hand equipment bay panel, down there to the - to the right of 251.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We have the CSM Lunar Landmark Map Book, which is about five-eighths of an inch thick.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: That should be satisfactory by itself.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We have a book in hand and ready to go.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: You want to place that across the base end of the - the probe assembly there, across the capture latch release handle; and this is going to be the padding that distributes the load a little bit from the base end of the probe assembly onto the paneling down there in the right-hand equipment bay, hatchwise from panel 252; so, you just want to tape it across the base there, so that it'll be sure and stay in position.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The book is taped to the base of the probe.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And what we'd like you to do next is to place a bag over the probe head, and the latest information shows that that liquid transfer demo hose bag probably will not fit; and, therefore, you can stow that either in Bravo-1 or - or back in Alpha-10; your option.

And we're recommending that you take an unused temporary stowage bag, that is, one that's not currently in use, and cut the mechanism off the top of the temporary stowage bag, place that over the probe head, and tape it in place.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: What we're trying to do here is protect the mechanism in the probe head against possible salt water or salt spray splashing in during the recovery.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We're just looking around; the temporary stowage bags are all filled at the moment.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Since you don't have anything else to do right now, why, we'd like you to press on to the TV attitude, 325, 090, and 000, and acquire on the High Gain when we get there.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Very little conversation coming down from the spacecraft at the present time.
The crew is involved in the Earth darkside dimlight photography.
95 019 miles out from the Earth approaching at a velocity of 5816 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We've got this bag that the - hatch -the hatch - window bracket for the camera, you know, they used on the zero phase, and we had as a stand by - 70-millimeter-camera hatch window adapter bag, and it's big enough to go over the - the probe head.
We just sweat it a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: If you can get that over and tape her down so that you have a water - salt water spray protection on the probe head, that's all we need.
transcript.speaker: McCandless
